mnemonics.title: DIPS - Demand Paging Steps
mnemonics.content: DIPS: Demand occurs → Interrupt (page fault) → Processor saves state → Swap needed? → Yes → Find free frame → Read from disk → Update page table → Restart instruction. Stands for: Demand Interrupt Processor Swap Find Read Update Restart

mnemonics.title: FABO - FIFO And Basic Order
mnemonics.content: FABO: Frames available, bring order. FIFO replaces the oldest page first - remember 'First In First Out' like a queue at a ticket counter.

mnemonics.title: LRU - Least Recently Used Memory
mnemonics.content: LRU: Like a librarian who remembers which books were least recently read and removes those first to make space for new requests.

mnemonics.title: VIPS - Valid Invalid Protection Share
mnemonics.content: VIPS: In page table, V=Valid (in memory), I=Invalid (not in memory), P=Protection bits, S=Share indicator. Remember: 'Valid In Pages Indicate Security'

mnemonics.title: THRASH - Thrashing Resolution Approach
mnemonics.content: THRASH: Thrashing occurs when too many page faults → High CPU utilization drops → System slows → Need to increase degree of multiprogramming or reduce working set. Remember: 'The High Activity Reduces System Health'

acronyms.usage: A special cache that stores recent virtual address to physical address translations to speed up memory access. Check TLB first before page table.
Hardware device that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses. Performs this translation using page tables.
Page replacement algorithm that removes the oldest page in memory. Simple but not always optimal. Think of a queue at a bank.
Page replacement algorithm that removes the page that hasn't been used for the longest time. Better performance than FIFO but requires hardware support.
Theoretical best page replacement algorithm that removes the page that won't be used for the longest time in future. Used as benchmark only.
Page replacement algorithm that classifies pages based on Reference (R) and Modify (M) bits, then removes lowest priority page.
Keeps track of which physical frames are free/allocated. Used during page fault handling to find an empty frame.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a chef's prep table (physical memory). Only ingredients currently being used are on the table. When a recipe needs a new ingredient not on the table, the chef goes to the pantry (disk) to fetch it - that's demand paging.
Think of a library study desk. You need a specific book (page) to study. If it's not on your desk (not in memory), you must request it from the library shelves (disk) - that's a page fault.

Imagine a building directory. Room numbers (virtual addresses) are mapped to actual room locations (physical addresses). Each entry tells you if the room is currently occupied (valid/invalid) and who can enter (protection).
A sticky note on your desk with frequently used phone numbers - quick access to translations you've looked up recently, saving time of checking the full directory.

Imagine running between two libraries 5 km apart to find books. You spend all your time traveling (swapping) rather than reading (processing). The system is thrashing when it's constantly swapping pages without doing useful work.
The set of books you typically have open on your desk while studying. This changes as you move between subjects. The OS tries to keep your current working set in memory to avoid page faults.

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant only stocking ingredients when orders come in, rather than pre-stocking everything. The kitchen (CPU) only gets ingredients (pages) when requested (demand), fetching from the storage room (disk) as needed.

Like asking for a book at a library counter, and the librarian says 'That book is in the stacks, let me go get it' - the book isn't at the ready shelf (memory) so they must fetch it from storage (disk).

Like a parking lot (physical memory) that's full. When a new car arrives (new page), the security guard (OS) must decide which car to ask to move - FIFO asks the car that arrived first, LRU asks the one that hasn't been used recently.

Like a student trying to study from 10 different textbooks but only has room for 1 on their desk. They constantly swap textbooks, spending more time swapping than studying. The system is overwhelmed with page transfers.
Like reading a book chapter. You stay on the current pages (spatial locality) and keep referring back to the same section repeatedly (temporal locality) before moving to the next chapter.

Think of pages as notebook pages. A clean page is like a photocopy - if thrown away, no information is lost. A dirty page is like your original notes - you must save them (write back to disk) before discarding.
